html:
Hedvig Elisabet Charlotta of Holstein-Gottorp, with the royal name Charlotta, born on March 22, 1759, and died on June 20, 1818, Duchess of Södermanland, was Queen of Sweden from 1809 to 1818 and Queen of Norway from 1814 to 1818. She was the daughter of Duke Frederick August I of Oldenburg and Friedrike of Hesse-Kassel, and a cousin of Gustav III and his brother Karl (XIII), whom she married on July 7, 1774.

Hedvig Elisabeth Charlotta is best known for her diary, which chronicles life at court from 1775 to 1817 and was published in 1902–1942. The diary, with its political commentary from the Queen herself, provides an invaluable insight into the center of power, and today it is one of the most valuable memoir works in Swedish literature, as well as a standard and reference work in Swedish historical research.

== Biography ==

=== Duchess ===

Hedvig Elisabet Charlotta was chosen as a bride for Karl of Gustav III in the hope of resolving the succession issue, as he had no children and his marriage was not yet complete. Karl noted during their first meeting (he was 22, she was eleven) in Eutin in 1770 that she was pretty, and the marriage was arranged in 1772.

The first wedding ceremony took place in Swedish Wismar on June 22, 1774. Before the wedding, she was welcomed in Wismar by a Swedish delegation consisting of her four ladies-in-waiting (Magdalena Ekeblad, Magdalena Strokirch, and "the Lewenhaupt sisters"), Hedvig Catharina von Fersen, and Sophie von Fersen, but without the lady-in-waiting Ulrika Charlotta Sprengtporten, accompanied by, among others, chamberlains Adolf Ludvig Piper and Adolf Ludwig Hamilton. She said goodbye to her Eutin retinue at Fürstenhof and was replaced by her Swedish entourage. Since the groom was absent, he was represented at the ceremony by the president of the Wismar tribunal, Baron Carl Otto von Höpken. After the wedding dinner, a reception was held for local dignitaries, followed by dinner for both the Swedish and Eutin entourages. She made a very good impression in Wismar, as she was not nervous or shy but immediately interacted with people she had never met before as if she had known them her whole life, and even spontaneously initiated a dance and musical evening.

She arrived in Dalarö, Sweden, on July 1 aboard the warship Sofia Albertina, landed at Erstavik on July 3, and was welcomed at the estate of councilman Drejer, Nyckelviken, by the female members of the royal family. She met the king at Stubbsund's meadow (near Duvnäs) in a gala carriage called Burmannia. She then arrived in Stockholm on July 7 via gondola, the so-called "royal sloop," where she was immediately formally presented to the queen, the dowager queen, members of the council, their wives, and the ambassadors. The second wedding ceremony took place that evening in the chapel at Stockholm Palace, followed by a masked ball in Kungsträdgården.

Hedvig Elisabet Charlotta was described as beautiful, lively, and articulate. She made a splash in social life upon her arrival and was initially called "Little Duchess" and later "Duchess Lotta." Riksråd Fredrik Sparre said about her: "One cannot imagine anything more cheerful and vivacious. She is pure joy. Her greatest pleasure is to joke and play pranks. It would be fortunate if she introduced that custom, as our naturally somewhat gloomy nation needs to be cheered up." However, she also received criticism for being frivolous and lacking dignity.

Through her acquaintance with Sophie von Fersen and her mother, she was able to establish a circle of acquaintances outside the court. She subsequently became part of the von Fersen family's circle and also worked politically with them. Since the queen, her sister-in-law, was not interested in court life, she became the central female figure at the court. In practice, she and her sister-in-law Sofia Albertina were responsible for the social life at the court as its hostesses, since Queen Sophia Magdalena only participated in social events when she had to. With her wit, charm, cheerfulness, and outspokenness, she embodied the ideal woman of the 18th century and was described as someone who could lighten the mood. She participated in the court's amateur theater, where she also danced, which was considered scandalous because dancing on stage during that era was associated with courtesans. After rumors spread that she and Sofia Albertina distracted the king from politics through their love of entertainment, she stopped participating in 1783. In May 1776, she was initiated by Karl, who led the Swedish Freemasons, into an adoption Masonic lodge. She became the leader of the Swedish female Freemasons. Other members included Sophie von Fersen, Hedvig Eleonora von Fersen, Ulrica Catharina Koskull, and likely Christina Charlotta Gyldenstolpe.

Her relationship with Karl was somewhat distant, and they lived more or less separate lives for many years. At the end of July 1775, Hedvig Elisabet Charlotta was declared pregnant, and prayers were held in the churches. According to Axel von Fersen the elder, it was the lady-in-waiting Sprengtporten who mistakenly identified a pregnancy. However, no signs of pregnancy appeared, even though she claimed to feel fetal movements.

How certain can we be about this?

Information about individuals from the 1800s is very well documented (through census records, newspapers, and letters). The uncertainty primarily concerns the selection of data and the perspective used – whose stories are being highlighted.
tldr: The text is about Queen Charlotte, a woman known for her vibrant personality and important diary. Her writings offer us today a unique glimpse into what life was like at the royal court in the past.
lattlast:
Queen Charlotte was born in 1759 and married King Charles XIII in 1774. She became Queen of Sweden and Norway during the early 1800s. She was often described as a cheerful and lively person who was very popular in social circles at the court, although some criticized her for being too frivolous.

She is particularly known for her diary, which she wrote between 1775 and 1817. This diary is now one of the most important historical texts in Sweden because it reveals how the center of power functioned and what the Queen herself thought about politics.

During her time as Queen, she was an important figure in the court's social activities. She participated in theater performances and was active in Freemasonry, which were organizations where people met to discuss ideas and build community.
